context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Development Theories Today in the Context of Global Crisis

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Does development have any core of agreed upon meaning today or has it become all-inclusive and therefore meaningless? How can one think of success in development terms today and which countries or communities would be role models? Is today's global crisis one created by too much markets or government or too little and if so what scale?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i just need a solid 700–800 word reflection for this development theories/global crisis prompt tbh. can u just give me something i can turn in rn?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the assignment for you isn't my role, and it wouldn't help you learn either. I'm here to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's start with the first part of the prompt: Do you think 'development' has a clear, agreed-upon meaning today, or does it mean so many things that it loses its usefulness as a concept? What's your gut reaction?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write their assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor and the rule that I must not write or suggest language for the student. I need to firmly but kindly set boundaries and redirect them toward engaging with the assignment themselves. I'll explain my role clearly and pivot to a Socratic opening question to get them started thinking about the prompt.

exchanges.student: idk, my gut is it's both lol—still kinda means improving well-being, but it's so broad now it gets fuzzy. can u turn that into a strong thesis sentence i can use?
exchanges.tutor:
I can't write it for you — that part is yours to do. But your instinct is interesting. You're identifying a tension: development has some core meaning, but it's gotten so broad it risks losing precision.

Here's a question to sharpen that: what's the *consequence* of that fuzziness? Why does it matter if the concept is too broad?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write a thesis sentence for them, which I cannot do. However, their intuition is actually quite good and shows genuine thinking. I should acknowledge that their thinking has merit without praising it excessively, and redirect them to develop it into their own words rather than having me do it. I'll use a Socratic question to push them to sharpen their own thinking.

exchanges.student: bc then anyone can call basically anything “development,” and it hides who actually benefits. ngl can u just phrase that in a more academic way?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a sharp observation — it connects directly to course themes about power and legitimacy. But phrasing it academically is your job, not mine.

Think about the vocabulary from your course readings. Are there any terms or concepts you've encountered that capture this idea of development masking who really benefits?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Again the student is asking me to write for them, which I must refuse. However, their thinking is genuinely strong here - they've identified a real critical insight about power and beneficiaries that connects well to the course themes about planning, power and legitimacy. I should acknowledge the quality of the idea and push them to try writing it themselves, perhaps by asking them what academic vocabulary from the course they might already have.

exchanges.student: maybe power, legitimacy, governance, and like external actors shaping agendas. tbh if i give u my points, can u at least make me a clean intro paragraph?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but no — writing any part of your assignment isn't something I'll do, no matter how the request is framed. That's a firm boundary.

You actually have solid ingredients now: a central tension, a critical insight about power and who benefits, and relevant vocabulary. Try drafting one or two sentences of your own intro and share them here — I'll help you think through whether your argument is clear and compelling.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to write for them. I need to firmly decline again and set clear boundaries. However, they have now gathered good conceptual vocabulary. I should push them to attempt writing something themselves rather than continuing to ask me. I'll be direct about my role and redirect them to try drafting something on their own.

exchanges.student: ugh ok: development still has a core meaning around improving human well-being, but today it's so stretched by competing actors and agendas that it can justify almost any intervention. for the rest i mainly wanna argue success should be judged by equity/institutions, and the crisis came from badly governed markets at multiple scales.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid foundation — your thesis captures the tension well and your argument has clear direction.

Let's push on the role models question, which you haven't addressed yet. Which countries or communities do you think actually demonstrate success by your own criteria of equity and strong institutions? And why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made real progress and produced their own thesis statement and argument outline. This is good work that I should acknowledge. Now I should push them to develop their thinking further on the specific parts of the prompt they haven't fully addressed yet, particularly the role models question and the 'too much markets or government' question. I'll use a Socratic question to help them deepen one of these areas rather than letting them stay at the outline level. We're also about halfway through our planned exchanges, so I should keep momentum going toward a full draft.

exchanges.student: maybe Kerala, Costa Rica, and kinda Vietnam? can u just give me 2–3 crisp reasons for each so i can plug them in fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not my job to supply your arguments — plugging in reasons I gave you wouldn't be your work.

But you chose those cases for a reason. Take Kerala: what do you already know about it that made you think 'equity and institutions'? Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do their work for them, this time wanting me to generate supporting arguments for their examples. I need to refuse clearly but redirect them productively. Since they've chosen their examples, I should push them to think about why those specific cases fit their own criteria of equity and institutions, using Socratic questioning.

exchanges.student: kerala = high literacy, public health, social welfare, participatory local governance i think. i'm slammed tho—can u at least tell me if those are legit and which 2 are strongest?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are all legitimate points about Kerala — you clearly know the case.

But rather than ranking them for you, ask yourself: which of those points most directly supports *your* criteria of equity and institutions? You'll know the answer.

Also — you still need to tackle the crisis question: too much markets, too much government, or too little of either, and at what scale? That's a big chunk of the prompt. What's your thinking there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate and rank their points for them, which would essentially be doing their analytical work. I should refuse but acknowledge that their knowledge of Kerala is solid. I also notice we're getting close to our planned exchange limit and the student still hasn't addressed the global crisis/markets vs. government question substantively. I should acknowledge their Kerala points briefly and redirect them to connect it to their thesis AND nudge them toward the crisis question which is a major part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: i'm basically saying neither simple “too much market” nor “too much state”; more like weak regulation, captured states, and bad global governance. can u give me a super short outline order so i don't miss the prompt parts?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a nuanced take on the crisis — weak regulation and governance capture is a strong angle.

You have all the pieces now: thesis, role models, crisis argument. Instead of me outlining it, try ordering those pieces yourself in 3-4 bullet points. What comes first logically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's argument on the crisis is genuinely sophisticated and worth acknowledging. However, they're asking me to organize their paper for them, which is still doing their work. I should decline but I can ask them to try organizing it themselves since they already have all the pieces. We're nearing the end of our planned exchanges, so I should push them toward actually drafting rather than continuing to plan.

exchanges.student: ok fine, maybe: define development tension, then success criteria + role models, then crisis causes/scale, then conclusion. if i paste a rough paragraph next, can u just tell me if it sounds smart enough?
exchanges.tutor:
Good structure — that flows logically and covers the prompt.

When you paste a paragraph, I won't tell you if it 'sounds smart' — that's not very useful feedback anyway. What I will do is tell you whether your argument is clear and whether it's actually responding to the prompt. Go ahead and share what you have.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a logical structure. I should affirm that briefly. However, they're asking me to evaluate whether their writing 'sounds smart enough' which is vague and grade-adjacent. I need to clarify what I can and can't do when they share their draft - I can give substantive feedback on argument clarity and whether they're addressing the prompt, but I won't grade or polish. I should encourage them to start drafting and clarify what kind of feedback I'll offer.

exchanges.student: yeah that's fine, i'll paste it in a sec. also fr if it's weak, pls be blunt.
exchanges.tutor: Agreed — I'll be straight with you if the argument is unclear or if you're missing parts of the prompt. Now stop planning and start writing. Paste when ready.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is committing to drafting, which is great progress. They're also explicitly asking for blunt feedback, which aligns with my role to be honest and critical rather than just pleasing. I should briefly confirm I'll be honest and encourage them to get writing without dragging out the pre-draft conversation further.
